The terrain around Adraskan — dry riverbeds, scattered compounds, open desert punctuated by irrigated valleys — was classic Afghan ambush country. Coalition and Afghan forces ran periodic clearing operations through the district, responding to intelligence about weapons caches, IED factories, or Taliban commanders transiting the area. Every operation followed the same rhythm: helicopter insertion or ground movement at dawn, cordon and search through the morning, contact in the afternoon when the fighters decided they'd observed long enough, extraction before dark. Adraskan wasn't on anyone's war map back home. But for the people who operated from Shindand, Adraskan was the backyard — close enough to reach, dangerous enough to respect, and representative of the quiet, grinding war that defined western Afghanistan.
belligerents: US Forces / Afghan National Army / ISAF
result: Periodic clearing operations — area remained contested

name: Arghandab Valley Operations
description: The Arghandab River Valley, north of Kandahar city. Orchards, pomegranates, grape rows, irrigation canals — lush, green, and saturated with IEDs. The 101st Airborne and later the 82nd Airborne fought here in some of the most intense close combat of the surge. The Taliban had seeded the valley so thoroughly with pressure-plate IEDs that companies were losing soldiers weekly to devices that couldn't be detected by electronic means — no metal, no wires, just a jug of homemade explosive and a wooden plate. Soldiers resorted to walking in single file, stepping exactly in the footprints of the man ahead. When a path was cleared, the Taliban would mine it again overnight. The pomegranate orchards provided perfect concealment for ambushes, and the mud-brick compound walls were thick enough to stop anything short of a tank round. Arghandab was the war at its most primal — men on foot, in orchards, stepping carefully, knowing that every step could be their last. No technology could save you. Just your eyes, your training, and whatever luck you had left.
result: Contested clearing operations — IED threat never fully eliminated

name: Operations in Nahr-e Saraj (Helmand)
description: Nahr-e Saraj — the district between Lashkar Gah and Gereshk in central Helmand. British and later US Marines turned this into the laboratory for population-centric counterinsurgency. Patrol bases every few kilometers. Daily foot patrols. Market engagement. Shuras with elders. And IEDs. God, the IEDs. Nahr-e Saraj was one of the most heavily mined areas in Afghanistan, and the British took a staggering number of amputations here. The compound patterns — walled agricultural plots connected by narrow paths through irrigated fields — created a tactical environment where every step was a decision and every decision could be your last leg. The Marines who took over brought more resources but faced the same problem: the Taliban could mine the ground faster than engineers could clear it. Nahr-e Saraj was where the counterinsurgency theory met the reality of an enemy who didn't need to fight you directly. They just needed to make the ground itself lethal. Walking. That's what it came down to. Soldiers walking through fields, trusting their training, their equipment, and their luck.
result: Gradual security improvement — heavy IED casualties throughout
commanders: British Task Force / US Marine commanders

name: Battle of Kandahar (Operation Hamkari)
description: 2010. Kandahar. The spiritual birthplace of the Taliban. Operation Hamkari — 'cooperation' — was the surge's main effort in the south, and it was unlike any previous operation. Instead of a dramatic D-Day assault, it was a slow squeeze: building security bubble by security bubble around Kandahar city, clearing the surrounding districts, and gradually choking off Taliban access to the population. The 101st Airborne, later the 4th Infantry Division, worked through Arghandab, Zhari, Panjwai, and the Horn of Panjwai — names that would fill casualty reports for months. Every operation was paired with governance engagement: district governors, provincial councils, development projects. The challenge in Kandahar was that the Taliban wasn't just an insurgency — they were the power structure. They were in the police. They were on the provincial council. They were the cousins and brothers of the men we were trying to empower. Kandahar was the war's most honest mirror: you can't defeat an enemy that is the government and the opposition simultaneously.
result: Temporary security improvement — Taliban influence never fully removed
commanders: MG James Terry / various brigade commanders

name: Battle of Sirte (2011)
description: For two months, Libyan rebels with NATO jets overhead hammered Gaddafi's last stronghold in his hometown of Sirte. When it finally fell, the man who'd styled himself the 'King of Kings of Africa' was dragged out of a drainage ditch, beaten, and killed on a cell phone camera. Forty-two years of megalomania ended in a concrete pipe. And then came the part nobody had planned for — what comes after the dictator. The answer, in Libya's case, was chaos that hasn't ended yet.

name: Operation Odyssey Dawn — Libya
description: French Rafales fired the first shots — destroying Gaddafi's tanks as they rolled toward Benghazi to slaughter the people who'd dared to revolt. Then the Tomahawks came, then the B-2s, then seven months of NATO bombing that flew over twenty-six thousand sorties and systematically dismantled Gaddafi's military until the rebels overran Tripoli. The intervention toppled a dictator, probably prevented a massacre, and created a failed state that's still burning. Libya is the cautionary tale that haunts every conversation about using airpower for regime change.

name: Operation Neptune Spear — Abbottabad Raid
description: Two stealth Black Hawks that nobody knew existed flew deep into Pakistani airspace without tripping a single radar — into the airspace of a nuclear-armed nation that was supposedly an ally. One helicopter crashed in the compound courtyard and the SEALs just kept going. Forty minutes later, Osama bin Laden was dead, and the team flew out on a backup Chinook. The crashed helicopter had stealth technology that the Chinese would be photographing within hours. The whole operation — the audacity of it, the technology, the risk — was the most dramatic covert air mission since Entebbe, and it ended a manhunt that had consumed a decade.

name: Operation Neptune Spear — Killing of Osama bin Laden
description: For nine years, eight months, and twenty days after September 11th, they hunted him. The trail that went cold at Tora Bora was picked up again through a courier — Abu Ahmed al-Kuwaiti — whose movements the CIA tracked to a compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan, a mile from Pakistan's military academy. On May 2, 2011, twenty-three Navy SEALs from DEVGRU flew two stealth Black Hawks across the Pakistani border without permission. One helicopter crashed in the compound courtyard — the mission nearly ended right there. The SEALs adapted and went in anyway. Room by room, floor by floor, up the stairs to the third floor. Bin Laden was shot and killed. 'Geronimo — EKIA.' In the White House Situation Room, the President and his team watched the feed in silence. There was no celebration. Just relief. The architect of 9/11, the man who changed the trajectory of a generation, was dead on a floor in Pakistan. They buried him at sea before dawn. The war didn't end. But something closed.

name: Extortion 17 Shootdown
description: On August 6, 2011, three months after the bin Laden raid, a CH-47D Chinook helicopter call sign Extortion 17 was shot down by an RPG in the Tangi Valley of Wardak Province. Thirty Americans died — seventeen of them Navy SEALs from the same unit that killed bin Laden, plus five Naval Special Warfare support personnel, three Air Force special tactics airmen, and five Army crew members. Five Afghan commandos and an Afghan interpreter were also killed. It was the single deadliest day for American forces in the entire Afghan war. Thirty-eight dead in a flash of light and falling metal. The Chinook was flying a quick reaction force to support an ongoing Army Ranger operation. Taliban fighters had been watching the landing zone. One man with a $100 rocket-propelled grenade killed more Americans in one second than al-Qaeda had in most entire years of the war. Gold Star families got the notification visits that night. Thirty of them. In one night.
belligerents: United States (Navy SEALs / Army / Air Force)
result: 38 killed (30 Americans) — deadliest single incident for US in Afghan war

name: Killing of Anwar al-Awlaki
description: On September 30, 2011, a CIA drone strike in Yemen's Al Jawf governorate killed Anwar al-Awlaki, the American-born radical cleric who had become al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula's most effective recruiter and operational planner. Born in New Mexico, educated at Colorado State and San Diego State, he spoke perfect English and used YouTube and the internet to radicalize a generation of Western jihadists. He inspired the Fort Hood shooter, the underwear bomber, the Charlie Hebdo attackers. He was the first American citizen deliberately targeted and killed by the US government without trial. The legal and moral questions were enormous and remain unsettled. His sixteen-year-old son, Abdulrahman — also an American citizen — was killed in a separate drone strike two weeks later. The GWOT had reached the point where the United States was executing its own citizens from the sky in countries it wasn't officially at war with. No one had planned for that on September 12, 2001.
result: Awlaki killed; raised lasting legal questions about extrajudicial killing of US citizens

name: US Withdrawal from Iraq
description: On December 18, 2011, the last American combat convoy — a column of armored MRAPs from the 3rd Brigade, 1st Cavalry Division — crossed the berm at the Khabari Crossing into Kuwait. They drove through the night to avoid attacks. Behind them: 4,487 Americans dead, over 32,000 wounded, hundreds of thousands of Iraqis killed, a trillion dollars spent, and a country that was fragile, fractured, and unsure of what it was. The Status of Forces Agreement had expired. The Iraqi government wouldn't grant immunity to US troops. So they left. Soldiers who had bled for Ramadi and Fallujah and Baghdad watched from home as the country they'd fought for slid toward the cliff. The Surge had worked, but no one stayed to hold the gains. The vacuum they left behind would be filled in three years by a group nobody had heard of yet — the Islamic State. Every Gold Star family was left with the same question: was it worth it? Nobody in Washington could answer.
result: Complete US military withdrawal from Iraq; gains subsequently eroded

name: Shindand Airbase Operations
description: Shindand. If you've never heard of it, you weren't in western Afghanistan. A sprawling airbase in Herat province, originally built by the Soviets, expanded by the Americans, surrounded by terrain that looked like Mars with scattered mud-brick villages. FOB Shindand was the hub for everything happening in RC-West — the Italian-led regional command that was supposed to be the 'quiet' part of Afghanistan. It wasn't quiet. The base took indirect fire regularly. The roads outside the wire were seeded with IEDs. The local power dynamics were a nested set of Russian dolls — warlords, tribal elders, Afghan National Army units of questionable loyalty, and Taliban shadow governors who ran their own parallel government. Living at Shindand meant existing in a reality that the news never covered. The western front of a war that the public had already forgotten. You breathed moon dust, you sweated through 120-degree summers, you pulled missions into valleys where the terrain itself was the enemy. Shindand wasn't famous. But for the people who served there, it was everything.
belligerents: US Forces / ISAF / Afghan National Security Forces
result: Ongoing operations — base served as key hub for western Afghanistan

name: Herat Province Operations
description: 2010-2012. Western Afghanistan. The part of the war nobody stateside talked about because Helmand and Kandahar were eating all the headlines and all the casualties. But Herat province had its own war — quieter, more complex, arguably more corrupt. The Italian-led Regional Command West ran the show, with US forces providing the sharp end when things got bad. And things got bad more often than the Italians wanted to admit. The Taliban had shadow governors in every district. The drug trade — opium from Farah and Nimroz provinces flowing through Herat to Iran — funded everything. The Afghan Local Police program was a double-edged sword: armed villagers who might fight the Taliban or might use their weapons to settle tribal grudges. For Americans at Shindand and the scattered bases across western Afghanistan, 2011-2012 was the war in its twilight — the surge in the south was winding down, the transition to Afghan lead was the official narrative, and everyone on the ground knew the Taliban was just waiting. Doing their pushups, as the saying went.
belligerents: US Forces / Italian ISAF / Afghan National Security Forces
result: Security gradually transferred to Afghan forces — contested stability

name: Zerkoh Valley Operations
description: The Zerkoh Valley in Ghor province — east of Shindand, accessible through mountain passes that might as well have been on another planet. This was Taliban transit territory: fighters, weapons, and opium moving between the southern and northern fronts through valleys that Alexander the Great would have recognized. Coalition operations here were sporadic and usually kinetic — you didn't go into the Zerkoh Valley for hearts and minds. You went in because intelligence said a weapons cache or a commander was there, and you hit fast and got out before the terrain ate you. Helicopter support was critical and often unavailable because of the altitude and weather. The Afghan National Army units that partnered on these missions ranged from capable to catastrophic depending on the unit and the day. For the Americans supporting these operations from Shindand, the Zerkoh Valley was the definition of expeditionary warfare in its rawest form — small teams, thin support, and mountain terrain that didn't care about your timeline.
result: Disruption operations — temporary effects on insurgent logistics

name: Italian ISAF Operations in RC-West
description: The Italians ran Regional Command West, and working with them was an education in coalition warfare — which is a diplomatic way of saying it was complicated. Italy had different rules of engagement, different political constraints, and a fundamentally different appetite for risk than the Americans. Their PRT in Herat city did solid development work. Their Carabinieri trained Afghan police. But when it came to offensive operations, the Italian national caveats — restrictions placed by Rome on what their forces could and couldn't do — created friction that everyone felt but nobody could officially acknowledge. The Americans at Shindand needed Italian coordination for operations in Herat province, and sometimes that coordination came fast and sometimes it didn't come at all. This isn't a knock on Italian soldiers — many of them were excellent, and they took casualties. It's the reality of coalition warfare: every nation has its own politics, its own red lines, its own definition of acceptable risk. The war in western Afghanistan was fought as much in coordination meetings as it was on the ground.
belligerents: Italian ISAF forces / US Forces / ANSF
result: Mixed — effective development work constrained by national caveats

name: Qala-i-Naw Operations (Badghis Province)
description: Badghis province. North of Herat, south of Turkmenistan, and firmly within the western Afghanistan area of operations that ran through Shindand. The Spanish PRT at Qala-i-Naw was the coalition's footprint in a province where the Taliban controlled the countryside and the government controlled the capital — barely. Spanish forces, with US support, ran clearing operations through districts that saw fighting spike during the 2010-2012 period as the Taliban pushed into areas previously considered stable. Badghis wasn't Helmand — the fighting was lower intensity but no less dangerous for the soldiers on patrol. IEDs, sniper fire, ambushes on convoys. The Spanish took casualties, and so did the Afghans. For Americans supporting operations from Shindand and forward bases, Badghis was part of the daily reality of western Afghanistan: a province that was too unstable to ignore and too vast to control.
belligerents: Spanish ISAF / US Forces / Afghan Army
result: Sustained security operations — gradual transition to Afghan lead

name: Nimroz Province Border Operations
description: Nimroz province. The southwestern corner of Afghanistan, bordering both Iran and Pakistan, making it a smuggler's paradise and a nightmare for border security. Drugs going out, weapons coming in, fighters crossing in both directions. The Afghan Border Police in Nimroz were among the most corrupt in the country — which is saying something. Operations from Shindand and other western bases regularly extended into Nimroz to interdict drug shipments and disrupt Taliban logistics. The Iranian border added a dimension that other theaters didn't have: Iranian intelligence services were active in western Afghanistan, playing all sides, supplying weapons to the Taliban while officially cooperating with the Afghan government. For US forces in RC-West, the Iranian angle was the subtext of everything. Every captured EFP, every intercepted shipment, every informant's report about 'visitors from the west' pointed toward Tehran. Nimroz was where the Afghan war, the drug war, and the shadow war with Iran all overlapped.
belligerents: US Forces / ISAF / Afghan Border Police
result: Ongoing interdiction operations — smuggling routes never fully disrupted

name: Extortion 17 Shootdown
description: August 6, 2011. Tangi Valley, Wardak province. A CH-47D Chinook — call sign Extortion 17 — was shot down by an RPG while inserting a quick reaction force to support Army Rangers in contact. Thirty Americans died, including seventeen members of Naval Special Warfare Development Group — the same unit that had killed Osama bin Laden three months earlier. Eight Afghan commandos and one interpreter also died. It was the deadliest single event of the entire twenty-year war. The helicopter was on approach to a landing zone in a valley where the enemy had the high ground — the oldest problem in mountain warfare, and the most lethal. One Taliban fighter with an RPG-7, a weapon designed in the 1960s, brought down a helicopter carrying some of the most elite operators in the world. The investigation found no systemic failure, just the brutal mathematics of war: put enough helicopters into enough valleys, and eventually the enemy gets lucky. For everyone who served in 2011, Extortion 17 was the day the war reached up and took the best we had.
result: 30 US and 8 Afghan personnel killed — deadliest single event of the war

name: Battle of Aleppo
description: Aleppo was one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in human history, and it took four years to destroy it. Rebels held the east, Assad held the west, and the line between them became a lunar landscape of barrel bombs, sniper fire, and starvation. When Russia intervened, the bombardment intensified to a level that made Grozny look restrained. By the time the last rebels evacuated eastern Aleppo in December 2016, five thousand years of civilization lay in rubble. That's Syria's civil war in one city.

name: Fall of Goma (2012)
description: M23 rebels — everyone knew Rwanda was behind them, nobody could prove it enough to matter — marched into Goma while 17,000 UN peacekeepers watched. The world's largest peacekeeping mission, with billions in funding, tanks, and helicopters, stood aside while a Rwandan proxy force captured a city of a million people. The Congolese army ran. The UN wrung its hands. Goma fell. It was the purest possible demonstration that peacekeeping without the will to fight is just expensive spectating.
result: M23 captured city; later withdrew under pressure

name: Benghazi Attack
description: On the eleventh anniversary of 9/11, Ansar al-Sharia militants stormed the US diplomatic compound in Benghazi, Libya with RPGs, machine guns, and mortars. Ambassador Chris Stevens and State Department officer Sean Smith were killed in the initial assault when the safe room filled with diesel smoke from fires the attackers set. Stevens was the first US ambassador killed in the line of duty since 1979. Hours later, a second attack hit the CIA annex a mile away. Former Navy SEALs Tyrone Woods and Glen Doherty — CIA contractors who had rushed to help — were killed by mortar fire on the annex roof while defending the facility. They had gone to the compound against orders to rescue survivors, then fought through the night at the annex. No military rescue force arrived. The politics consumed Washington for years afterward. But in Benghazi that night, four Americans died — two of them running toward the guns because that's what they did.
result: 4 Americans KIA including Ambassador Stevens; compound destroyed

name: Camp Bastion Attack
description: On September 14, 2012, fifteen Taliban fighters wearing US Army uniforms breached the perimeter wire at Camp Bastion in Helmand Province — one of the largest and supposedly most secure bases in Afghanistan. They split into three teams and attacked the airfield with RPGs, automatic weapons, and suicide vests. They destroyed six AV-8B Harrier jets — $200 million in aircraft — and damaged two more, effectively crippling the Marine Harrier squadron. It was the worst loss of US military aircraft on the ground since the Tet Offensive in 1968. Lieutenant Colonel Christopher Raible, the Harrier squadron commander, and Sergeant Bradley Atwell were killed fighting the attackers on the flight line. Raible grabbed a rifle and charged into the fight to protect his Marines and his aircraft. The attack lasted four hours. Fourteen of the fifteen attackers were killed, one captured. The Taliban had walked through the wire of a major coalition base. Nowhere was safe.
result: 2 Marines KIA; 6 Harrier jets destroyed; 14 of 15 attackers killed

name: Attack on Camp Bastion
description: September 14, 2012. Fifteen Taliban fighters in US Army uniforms cut through the perimeter wire of Camp Bastion — the massive British/American base in Helmand province — and went straight for the flight line. They destroyed six AV-8B Harrier jump jets. Six. That's more than $200 million in aircraft, and it was the greatest loss of American military aircraft in a single incident since the Tet Offensive in 1968. Two Marines were killed, including Lieutenant Colonel Christopher Raible, the squadron commander, who charged into the fight to defend his aircraft and his Marines. The Taliban had rehearsed the assault for weeks, using a mockup of the base. They knew exactly where to go. The breach exposed a complacency that had crept into base security — the assumption that the big FOBs were untouchable. Prince Harry was on the base at the time, which made international headlines, but the real story was simpler and more devastating: the Taliban walked onto the largest coalition base in southern Afghanistan and wreaked havoc. In 2012. Eleven years into the war.
result: Attack repelled — 6 Harriers destroyed, 2 Marines KIA

name: Battle of al-Qusayr
description: When Hezbollah rolled into al-Qusayr, the masks came off. A Lebanese militia was now fighting an expeditionary war in a foreign country to save a dictator. Hezbollah's fighters were battle-hardened, disciplined, and far more effective than Assad's own army — they cleared al-Qusayr in weeks and secured the supply lines from Lebanon that kept the regime alive. The guerrilla force that fought Israel in 2006 had become something new: a regional army-for-hire, and Iran's most effective foreign policy tool.

name: Battle of Juba (2013)
description: South Sudan was two years old — the world's youngest country, born out of decades of suffering — and its president and vice president turned their guns on each other. Fighting erupted in Juba between Dinka and Nuer soldiers who were supposed to be in the same army, and within hours a political dispute became an ethnic war. Nearly 400,000 people would die. Millions displaced. The newest nation on Earth tearing itself apart before it had even learned to stand. Hope is a fragile thing in that part of the world.
result: Kiir retained capital; civil war began

name: Operation Serval (Mali)
description: Jihadist columns were rolling south toward Bamako and the Malian army was evaporating when France did something that nobody expected — it acted fast. Within days, French jets were hitting jihadist convoys in the desert and the Foreign Legion was rolling into Timbuktu. The whole thing was over in weeks. Timbuktu liberated, Gao retaken, the jihadists scattered into the Sahara. It was the most effective Western military intervention in Africa in decades, and proof that speed and decisiveness can accomplish what years of half-measures can't.

name: Battle of Konna
description: January 2013. The jihadists controlling northern Mali pushed south — toward Bamako, the capital. Konna was the last town before the line of no return. If Konna fell, the Malian military had nothing between the jihadists and a capital city of two million people. The Malian army fought and lost. Konna fell in hours. The Malian government begged France for help, and Paris responded with Operation Serval — Mirage fighters, helicopters, paratroopers, and Foreign Legionnaires flooding into Mali in a matter of days. French helicopter attacks stopped the jihadi advance at Konna. It was the opening shot of France's Sahel war, the intervention that would eventually become Operation Barkhane, and the reason there were American forces in Niger when Tongo Tongo happened. Konna was the moment the world realized that the collapse of Libya in 2011 had flooded the Sahel with weapons and fighters, and that a new front in the war on terror had opened in the vast emptiness of West Africa.
belligerents: Malian Armed Forces / French Operation Serval
result: Jihadi advance halted by French intervention
commanders: Iyad Ag Ghali (Ansar Dine) / AQIM leaders

name: Battle of Gao
description: February 2013. French and Malian forces liberated Gao — the largest city in northern Mali — from the jihadist coalition that had held it for nine months. MUJAO fighters had imposed sharia law, amputated hands, stoned people, and destroyed anything they considered un-Islamic. The French retook the city quickly, but the jihadists didn't leave — they switched to guerrilla warfare. Car bombs, suicide attacks, and infiltration into the city from the surrounding desert. A car bomb at a checkpoint. A suicide attacker at the police station. The battle for Gao wasn't a single fight; it was the transition from conventional liberation to the asymmetric grind that would define the Sahel conflict for the next decade. For anyone who deployed to the region, Gao was the proof that taking territory back was the easy part. Holding it — governing it — that's where wars are won or lost. The Sahel hasn't figured that out yet.

name: Battle of Kobani
description: The whole world watched Kobani die on live television — Turkish camera crews on the hilltops filming ISIS black flags advancing through the streets. It looked over. Then the American airstrikes started, and the Kurdish fighters — men and women, some of them teenagers — refused to give up. Block by block, building by building, they pushed ISIS back in the kind of close urban combat that neutralizes every advantage an attacker has. Kobani was where ISIS learned it could be beaten, and where the Kurdish fighters became the most unlikely heroes of the war.
warName: Syrian Civil War / War against ISIS

name: Fall of Mosul (2014)
description: Eight hundred jihadists in pickup trucks routed thirty thousand Iraqi soldiers in four days. Thirty thousand. Soldiers stripped off their uniforms, dropped their American-made weapons, and ran. ISIS rolled through Mosul virtually unopposed, and al-Baghdadi climbed the pulpit of the Great Mosque to declare a caliphate. It was the most jaw-dropping military collapse since the fall of France — a billion dollars in US-supplied equipment captured, a city of two million seized, and the entire post-Iraq War order shattered in less than a week.

name: Operation Protective Edge (Gaza 2014)
description: The tunnels changed everything. Hamas had built an underground network beneath Gaza that would've impressed the Viet Cong — attack tunnels reaching into Israel, supply tunnels, command bunkers, weapons caches. Israel went in for fifty days to destroy them, and the fighting above ground was devastating. 2,200 Palestinians dead, 73 Israelis dead, entire neighborhoods flattened. Israel destroyed tunnels. Hamas dug new ones. The whole thing had the sickening feeling of a dress rehearsal for something worse.

name: Annexation of Crimea
description: They appeared overnight — soldiers in Russian uniforms with no insignia, surrounding Ukrainian bases, blocking roads, seizing government buildings. The 'little green men.' Not a single shot fired. Within weeks, Crimea held a sham referendum and Russia annexed it. The fastest, most bloodless territorial grab since Hitler took the Sudetenland, and achieved with the same playbook: fait accompli, deny everything, dare the world to do something about it. The world imposed sanctions. Putin kept Crimea. The post-Cold War order died on a peninsula in the Black Sea.

name: Battle of Ilovaisk
description: Ukrainian volunteer battalions were pushing toward Ilovaisk, rolling up separatist positions, when suddenly the troops shooting back weren't separatists anymore. They were Russian regulars — T-72B3 tanks, BM-21 Grad rockets, professional soldiers. The Ukrainians were encircled, offered a 'corridor' to withdraw, and then massacred as they tried to use it. The 'corridor of death' killed or captured a thousand Ukrainians and shattered any illusion that Russia wasn't directly fighting in Donbas. The Minsk ceasefire that followed was signed under the gun.
casualties: ~1,000 Ukrainian KIA/captured in the 'corridor of death'

name: Battle of Donetsk Airport
description: For 242 days, Ukrainian soldiers held the Donetsk airport against everything Russia and the separatists could throw at them. The terminal collapsed around them. They fought from the rubble. Floors fell in. They fought from the basement. The separatists started calling them 'Cyborgs' because nothing seemed to kill them. The airport was eventually lost — there was nothing left to hold but a pile of concrete — but the legend of the Cyborgs became the founding myth of Ukraine's resistance. Eight years later, when Russia invaded, every Ukrainian soldier knew the story.

name: Coalition Air War Against ISIS
description: ISIS carved out a caliphate the size of Britain, and the Coalition spent five years bombing it back into the sand. Thirty-four thousand strikes across Iraq and Syria — Reapers circling for hours waiting for a target to step outside, JDAMs turning vehicle-bomb factories into craters, special operators on rooftops in Mosul painting targets with lasers while F-15Es dropped ordnance danger-close. It was the most sustained precision air campaign in history, and it worked — the physical caliphate was erased. But you can't bomb an idea, and the ideology that built ISIS is still out there.

name: Fall of Mosul to ISIS
description: In June 2014, approximately 1,500 ISIS fighters rolled into Mosul — Iraq's second-largest city — and 30,000 Iraqi Army soldiers dropped their American-supplied weapons, stripped off their uniforms, and ran. Four divisions dissolved overnight. Billions of dollars in US military equipment — Humvees, MRAPs, artillery pieces, ammunition — fell into ISIS hands. The Iraqi Army that the United States had spent eight years and $25 billion building evaporated like smoke. ISIS declared a caliphate from the Grand al-Nuri Mosque. Their black flags flew over a city of two million people. For every American who had served in Iraq, who had trained those units, who had fought and bled and lost friends building an Iraqi military, it was a gut punch beyond words. Everything. All of it. Every patrol, every firefight, every friend killed — and thirty thousand soldiers ran from fifteen hundred fanatics. The war wasn't over. It had never ended. It had just been waiting.
result: ISIS captured Mosul; Iraqi Army routed; caliphate declared

name: Siege of Baiji Oil Refinery
description: The Baiji oil refinery — Iraq's largest, processing 300,000 barrels per day — became a ten-month battlefield between Iraqi forces and ISIS from June 2014 to May 2015. ISIS besieged the facility repeatedly, and the few hundred Iraqi soldiers inside held on in conditions that defied belief. Surrounded, resupplied only by helicopter, fighting off wave after wave of suicide vehicle bombs, they held the refinery when everything around them had fallen. US airstrikes hammered ISIS positions daily. The refinery changed hands multiple times. By the time Iraqi forces finally secured it, the facility was a charred skeleton — destroyed beyond economic repair. ISIS had proven they could paralyze Iraq's economy by threatening a single piece of infrastructure. The defenders at Baiji proved that some Iraqi soldiers would fight. They just needed leadership, support, and a reason. The refinery that supplied a third of Iraq's fuel was gone, but the stand there kept ISIS from claiming total dominance of northern Iraq.
belligerents: Iraqi Security Forces / US-led Coalition airpower
result: Iraqi forces held; refinery destroyed beyond use

name: ISIS Seizure of Kirkuk Area
description: June 2014. The Iraqi Army collapsed. Not retreated — collapsed. Divisions that the US had spent a decade training, equipping, and mentoring dropped their weapons and ran. ISIS — the mutant offspring of al-Qaeda in Iraq — swept out of Mosul and poured south. They reached the outskirts of Kirkuk. And then something happened that every veteran of northern Iraq understood in their bones: the Peshmerga stepped in. The Kurdish fighters moved into the vacuum, seized Kirkuk city, and drew a line. They held it, but barely. ISIS fighters were in Hawija — of course they were in Hawija — and the villages south and west of Kirkuk became a no-man's-land. For anyone who'd served in Kirkuk province, watching this unfold on CNN was a special kind of hell. Those roads you drove. Those checkpoints you manned. Those markets where you bought chai. All of it overrun by a medieval death cult wearing black flags. The places you bled to secure, lost in a week. That's the part that keeps you up at night.
result: Kirkuk city held by Peshmerga — surrounding areas contested

name: Siege of Kobani
description: September 2014 to January 2015. Kobani, on the Syrian-Turkish border. ISIS threw everything they had at this Kurdish city — tanks, artillery, suicide bombers, thousands of fighters. The world watched on live television as Turkish citizens stood on hilltops across the border and watched the city burn. The Kurdish YPG/YPJ fighters — including female fighters who terrified ISIS because they believed being killed by a woman denied them paradise — held block by block with small arms and courage that defied logic. US airstrikes, eventually authorized after weeks of hand-wringing, turned the tide. Over 700 airstrikes in four months, the most concentrated air campaign since the invasion of Iraq. ISIS lost an estimated 2,000 fighters at Kobani — their first major strategic defeat. The battle proved that ISIS could be beaten, and it forged the US-Kurdish partnership that would eventually retake Raqqa. For every veteran of northern Iraq who'd worked alongside Kurds, Kobani hit close to home. Those were the same people — Kurds fighting for their existence, again, with minimal support, again.
result: Kurdish / Coalition victory — ISIS first major strategic defeat

name: Battle of Baiji Refinery
description: 2014-2015. The Baiji oil refinery — Iraq's largest — sat on the road between Baghdad and Kirkuk, and ISIS wanted it. The siege lasted over a year. Iraqi security forces held the refinery complex while ISIS controlled the surrounding town, turning every resupply attempt into a running battle. Convoys pushing north from Tikrit had to fight through ISIS checkpoints, IEDs, and ambushes just to deliver food and ammunition to the garrison. Iraqi soldiers held that refinery through heat, hunger, and constant attack because losing Baiji meant losing Iraq's oil infrastructure and the highway north to Kirkuk. When the complex was finally secured in late 2015, the refinery was destroyed — damaged beyond repair by the fighting. Iraq 'won' Baiji but lost the refinery that was the reason for fighting there. For northern Iraq veterans, Baiji was on the road you drove. The refinery was the landmark you navigated by. Watching it burn on satellite imagery felt like watching your own history go up in smoke.
belligerents: Iraqi Security Forces / Shia militia / Coalition air
result: Iraqi forces hold — refinery destroyed in fighting

name: Sinjar Massacre and Siege
description: August 2014. ISIS came to Sinjar, and they came to exterminate. The Yazidis — a religious minority that ISIS declared 'devil worshippers' — had nowhere to run. The Peshmerga who were supposed to protect them withdrew without fighting. Thousands of Yazidis fled to Mount Sinjar in temperatures exceeding 120 degrees. On the plains below, ISIS was executing men by the hundreds and enslaving women and girls. This wasn't war — this was genocide. The UN would later formally recognize it as such. US airstrikes and airdrops kept the people on the mountain alive, and a corridor was eventually opened for them to escape through Syria. But what ISIS did in Sinjar — the mass graves, the slave markets, the systematic destruction of a people — was the act that changed the war. It was the reason the US came back to Iraq. Not for oil, not for politics, but because what was happening was an atrocity that demanded a response. For anyone who'd served in northern Iraq, who'd worked with Yazidi interpreters, who'd driven through Sinjar district, the images from August 2014 were personal in a way that transcended professional military concern. Those were people you knew.
belligerents: Yazidi civilians / Kurdish YPG/PKK / US air support
result: Genocide of Yazidis — international intervention prevents total massacre

name: Battle of Tikrit (2015)
description: Tikrit was Saddam's hometown, and now it was ISIS territory, and the force coming to take it back was led by Iranian-backed Shiite militias flying religious banners. The irony was almost too thick to process. Thirty thousand troops rolled in and the fighting was real, but the political subtext was louder than the gunfire — Iran was becoming the dominant military player in Iraq, and everyone in Washington could see it happening and couldn't figure out how to stop it.

name: Battle of Aden (2015)
description: The Houthis rolled into Aden and trapped the Saudi-backed government in its last major city. Then Saudi Arabia decided to intervene, and the result was months of urban warfare that turned a beautiful port city into a wasteland. Saudi coalition jets, Emirati armor, Yemeni militias — all grinding through Aden's streets against Houthi fighters who didn't care about the coalition's technological advantage because the ranges were too close for it to matter. The city was 'liberated.' Most of it was also destroyed.

name: Sinjar Offensive
description: A year earlier, ISIS had swept into Sinjar and committed genocide against the Yazidi people — mass executions, women and girls sold as slaves, an ancient religious community nearly erased from the earth. When the Kurdish Peshmerga and PKK fighters came back with American air support, they found the mass graves. They found the slave markets' records. They found the evidence of a horror so systematic and so deliberate that it will be prosecuted as genocide for decades to come. Sinjar was liberated. What happened to the Yazidis can never be undone.

name: Battle of Debaltseve
description: While diplomats in Minsk were literally signing the ceasefire agreement, Russian and separatist forces were tightening the noose around Debaltseve. The ink wasn't dry on Minsk II before the assault went in. Ukrainian troops fought their way out through a gauntlet of fire in a chaotic withdrawal that left equipment and dead scattered across the frozen roads. Putin's message couldn't have been clearer: paper agreements mean nothing when the other side has tanks and the will to use them.

name: Fall of Kunduz (2015)
description: The Taliban walked into Kunduz and raised their flag over the first provincial capital to fall since 2001. Fourteen years of American war, training, and money, and the Afghan army couldn't hold a major city for a single day. They retook it in two weeks with US airstrikes — including one that hit a Doctors Without Borders hospital, killing 42 people. Kunduz was a prophecy: the Afghan government was hollow, the Taliban was patient, and the ending everyone feared was already written. It just took six more years to arrive.
result: Taliban briefly captured; recaptured by Afghan/US forces

name: Baga Massacre / Battle of Baga
description: Boko Haram swept through Baga and killed an estimated 2,000 people — men, women, children, anyone they could find. Satellite imagery showed entire neighborhoods burned to the ground. It was the deadliest single attack in the group's blood-soaked history, and the world barely noticed because the Charlie Hebdo shooting in Paris happened the same week. Twelve people died in Paris and the world marched. Two thousand died in Baga and the world shrugged. The arithmetic of global attention has never been more brutally exposed.
result: Boko Haram captured town; later recaptured by Nigerian forces

name: Russian Air Campaign in Syria
description: Russia said they were bombing ISIS. They were mostly bombing everyone else — the rebels around Aleppo, Idlib, Homs, anyone threatening Assad's grip on power. Su-34s and Su-25s dropping unguided bombs on hospitals and apartment blocks while Moscow called them 'precision strikes against terrorist infrastructure.' The campaign was brutal, indiscriminate, and devastatingly effective. Assad's regime, which had been on the verge of collapse, survived. Russia got a warm-water military base and a testing ground for every new weapon in its arsenal. The price was paid in Syrian blood.
result: Assad regime stabilized; rebel forces degraded

name: Saudi-Led Air Campaign in Yemen
description: Saudi F-15s loaded with American precision munitions bombed hospitals. They bombed weddings. They bombed a school bus full of children. They had total air supremacy over Yemen — the Houthis couldn't put a single aircraft in the sky — and they used it to create the worst humanitarian catastrophe of the twenty-first century. Twenty-three thousand dead, millions starving, and not one clear strategic objective achieved. Yemen is the definitive case study in what happens when you hand unlimited airpower to a military with no strategy, no restraint, and no accountability.

name: Killing of Jihadi John (Mohammed Emwazi)
description: On November 12, 2015, a US drone strike in Raqqa, Syria vaporized a vehicle carrying Mohammed Emwazi — known to the world as Jihadi John. The British-Kuwaiti ISIS executioner had appeared in multiple hostage beheading videos, always dressed in black, face covered, speaking in a London accent while he murdered journalists James Foley, Steven Sotloff, David Haines, Alan Henning, Peter Kassig, and Japanese hostages Kenji Goto and Haruna Yukawa. His videos were designed to terrify the Western world, and they worked. He became the most recognizable face of ISIS's savagery — a face nobody had actually seen. When the drone found him, he was walking to a vehicle in downtown Raqqa. The Hellfire missile didn't leave much to identify. The man who had stood over bound prisoners and lectured the camera while holding a knife was reduced to DNA fragments. The hostage families got a phone call. It wasn't justice. But it was something.
result: Mohammed Emwazi killed in drone strike

name: Boko Haram — Lake Chad Basin Operations
description: The Lake Chad Basin — where Nigeria, Niger, Chad, and Cameroon meet — became Boko Haram's killing ground. By 2015, Boko Haram controlled territory the size of Belgium, had killed tens of thousands, displaced over two million people, and kidnapped 276 girls from Chibok that the whole world tweeted about and then forgot. The Multinational Joint Task Force — Nigerian, Nigerien, Chadian, and Cameroonian soldiers with US, French, and British support — launched a grinding campaign to retake the region. The fighting was brutal: Boko Haram used child suicide bombers, slaughtered entire villages, and fought with a savagery that shocked even hardened soldiers. For Americans deployed to Niger, the Boko Haram threat to the southeast was the other war — the one happening just across the border from where you slept. The explosions you sometimes heard weren't thunder. The refugees streaming through your area of operations weren't fleeing drought. The Lake Chad conflict was and remains one of the worst humanitarian disasters on earth, and almost nobody in America could find it on a map.
result: Boko Haram territory reduced but group not defeated — ongoing

name: Liberation of Tikrit from ISIS
description: March 2015. Tikrit again. The same city the 4th Infantry Division took in 2003. Now it was Iraqi forces — regular army, Shia militias, and eventually US air support — retaking it from ISIS. The operation was controversial from the start: Iranian-backed Shia militias, including some the US had fought against in Baghdad, were the primary ground force. Qassem Soleimani was photographed on the front lines, directing operations. The city was rigged with IEDs — ISIS had turned Tikrit into a bomb factory, and every building was potentially wired. The coalition initially refused to provide air support because of the militia presence. When the ground assault stalled, Iraq had to choose: Iranian support or American support. They chose American air power, the militias pulled back, and the Iraqi army pushed through. Tikrit was liberated, but the political dynamics of who fought for it and who controlled it afterward foreshadowed the fractured, militia-dominated Iraq that would emerge from the ISIS war.
belligerents: Iraqi Army / Shia PMF militias / Coalition air
result: Iraqi victory — Tikrit liberated from ISIS
commanders: Iraqi Joint Operations Command / Shia militia leaders

name: Kunduz — MSF Hospital Airstrike
description: October 3, 2015. A US AC-130 gunship circled over Kunduz and systematically destroyed a Medecins Sans Frontieres trauma hospital for over an hour. Forty-two people killed — patients, doctors, nurses. The crew received coordinates that they were told was a Taliban command post. The hospital had shared its GPS coordinates with all parties to the conflict. The strike continued even after MSF frantically called US and Afghan officials reporting they were being bombed. The investigation found a cascade of human error and system failure, but 'error' doesn't cover it for the families of the dead. Kunduz was the moment that tested what America says about itself — about accountability, about the laws of war, about the value of human life. No criminal charges were filed. For anyone who served in Afghanistan, who called in air support, who trusted the system that was supposed to prevent exactly this — Kunduz is a wound. Because every one of us knows we were one bad coordinate, one miscommunication, one exhausted JTAC away from our own Kunduz.
belligerents: US Air Force AC-130 / Afghan Ground Forces
commanders: N/A — hospital was the target in error

name: Fall and Liberation of Ramadi
description: May 2015 — ISIS takes Ramadi. The capital of Anbar province, the city that the Marines and Awakening tribes bled to save in 2006-2007, falls to a force of barely a thousand ISIS fighters using thirty VBIEDs — vehicle-borne IEDs — as rolling artillery. The Iraqi Army's 8th Brigade disintegrates. Soldiers flee in their underwear. The ghosts of every American who fought and died for Ramadi are screaming. Then, December 2015: Iraqi Counter-Terrorism Service, backed by coalition airstrikes, begins the liberation. Compound by compound, block by block, through a city ISIS has spent seven months rigging with the most sophisticated IED defenses in the history of warfare. The ICTS — Iraq's Golden Division — takes devastating casualties. But they don't break. They push through. Ramadi is liberated by January 2016. For every OIF veteran, the fall and liberation of Ramadi is the cruelest lesson: you can win a city, hand it to allies, and watch it fall again within a decade. The same real estate, bought twice with blood.
result: Ramadi liberated — city heavily damaged, thousands displaced

name: Battle of Mosul (2016-2017)
description: It took ISIS four days to take Mosul. It took a hundred thousand Iraqi soldiers, backed by the most powerful air force on Earth, nine months to take it back. The Old City was the worst of it — medieval streets too narrow for vehicles, ISIS snipers in every minaret, suicide bombers around every corner, and a quarter million civilians trapped in the middle. By the end, the Old City looked like Dresden. Tens of thousands of civilians dead. The caliphate destroyed, but at a cost that should keep every war planner awake at night.
casualties: ~1,500 Iraqi security forces KIA; thousands of ISIS KIA; ~9,000-11,000 civilians killed

name: Battle of Manbij
description: Manbij was ISIS's gateway to Turkey — the pipeline through which foreign fighters, money, and supplies flowed into the caliphate. The SDF spent 73 days cutting it off, fighting through a city where ISIS used civilians as human shields and booby-trapped every building. American special operators were close enough to the fighting to taste the dust. When Manbij fell, ISIS's lifeline to the outside world was severed. The caliphate was now a closed box, and the walls were closing in.
warName: Syrian Civil War / War against ISIS

name: Recapture of Palmyra (2016)
description: ISIS had turned Palmyra into a stage for its most theatrical brutality — executing prisoners in the Roman amphitheater, dynamiting 2,000-year-old temples, erasing history because their ideology couldn't tolerate it. When Syrian troops and Russian special forces took it back, the Temple of Bel was rubble and the archeological treasures of a civilization were dust. ISIS retook the city months later, because that's how the Syrian war worked — you could liberate the same ruin twice and still not win.

name: Battle of Mosul (Liberation)
description: The operation to retake Mosul from ISIS began in October 2016 and became the largest military operation in Iraq since the 2003 invasion. One hundred thousand Iraqi troops, Kurdish Peshmerga, and Shia militia forces, backed by US special operations advisors and relentless coalition airpower, fought to liberate a city of nearly two million people that ISIS had held for over two years. The east side took three months. The west side — the old city with its narrow streets and ancient buildings — took another five months of fighting that destroyed entire neighborhoods. ISIS used human shields, car bombs, tunnels, and snipers. The Iraqi Counter-Terrorism Service, the Golden Division, fought with extraordinary courage and took devastating casualties. When it was finally declared liberated in July 2017, the old city was rubble. The Great Mosque of al-Nuri, where Baghdadi had declared his caliphate, had been blown up by ISIS as they retreated. An estimated 40,000 civilians were killed.
belligerents: Iraqi Security Forces / Kurdish Peshmerga / US-led Coalition
result: Mosul liberated after 9 months; ISIS caliphate broken; city devastated
commanders: Lt Gen Abdul Amir Yarallah / US advisors

name: Killing of Abu Omar al-Shishani
description: In March 2016, a US airstrike targeted Abu Omar al-Shishani — 'Omar the Chechen' — the red-bearded Georgian former soldier who had become ISIS's most effective military commander. A veteran of the 2008 Russo-Georgian War, Shishani brought actual conventional military experience to an organization that mostly ran on fanaticism and car bombs. He planned and led major ISIS offensives across Syria and Iraq, and the Pentagon called him their 'minister of war.' He was critically wounded in the airstrike near al-Shaddadi, Syria and died of his injuries in July 2016. Shishani represented the most dangerous type of foreign fighter — not a radicalized amateur, but a trained soldier who knew how to plan operations, move forces, and exploit terrain. His death degraded ISIS's military capability at a critical moment, when the coalition was preparing the campaigns to retake Mosul and Raqqa.
result: Al-Shishani killed; ISIS military leadership degraded

name: Diffa Region Operations (Niger-Nigeria Border)
description: Diffa, southeastern Niger. The frontline against Boko Haram. The region had been under a state of emergency since 2015, with Boko Haram fighters crossing from northeastern Nigeria to attack villages, markets, and refugee camps. The Nigerien military, supported by US training and intelligence, fought a brutal counterinsurgency against an enemy that specialized in terrorizing civilians — kidnapping, forced recruitment of children, and suicide bombings in crowded markets. US Special Forces trained Nigerien units that deployed to Diffa, and US ISR assets provided the eyes in the sky. For Americans deployed to Niger, Diffa was the reminder that the jihadi threat wasn't just in the Sahara to the north — it was also pressing in from the southeast, a two-front problem for a country with limited resources and unlimited frontage. The human suffering in Diffa — hundreds of thousands of displaced people, entire villages depopulated — was staggering and invisible to the outside world.

name: Liberation of Fallujah from ISIS
description: May-June 2016. Fallujah. Again. Third time. The city that American Marines took in 2004, twice, at the cost of 95 dead and over a thousand wounded, had been under ISIS control since January 2014 — longer than any other Iraqi city. Iraqi security forces and Shia militias surrounded the city and pushed in. The fighting was fierce but the outcome was never really in doubt — by 2016, ISIS was being compressed on all fronts. What was in doubt was what would happen to the Sunni civilians trapped inside. Reports of militia atrocities against fleeing civilians filtered out. Mass detention sites. Disappearances. The sectarian dynamic that had fueled the insurgency in the first place was being replayed under different management. For every Marine who bled in Fallujah's streets in 2004, watching the 2016 liberation was a complicated kind of anguish. The city was being freed, yes. But by forces that might treat its people little better than the occupiers they replaced. Fallujah's curse is that it's always being fought over, and the people who live there always pay the price.
belligerents: Iraqi Security Forces / Shia PMF / Coalition air
result: Fallujah liberated — civilian casualties and militia abuses reported

name: Battle of Raqqa
description: Raqqa was where ISIS beheaded hostages on camera, where they threw gay men off buildings, where they built the machinery of a terrorist state. The SDF — mostly Kurdish fighters backed by American special operators and airpower — took it apart in four months of urban warfare that left the city looking like a parking lot. The caliphate's capital fell. The symbolism was enormous. But the rubble and the bodies underneath it told a different story about the cost of liberation.
warName: Syrian Civil War / War against ISIS

name: Siege of Deir ez-Zor
description: For three years, the Syrian garrison at Deir ez-Zor held out against ISIS — completely surrounded, supplied only by airdrops, eating whatever they could find, fighting off assaults that came from every direction. Three years. Think about that. When Russian-backed Syrian forces finally broke through the siege lines in September 2017, the defenders had been living in a real-life Beau Geste for over a thousand days. The commander, Issam Zahreddine, became a legend. He was killed by a mine two months later.

name: Battle of Tal Afar (2017)
description: Three years after 800 ISIS fighters routed 30,000 Iraqi soldiers at Mosul, the Iraqi army took Tal Afar in eleven days. Eleven days. The same army that had dropped its weapons and run was now executing combined-arms operations with competence that would've been unthinkable in 2014. Tal Afar was proof that armies can learn from catastrophe — and that the Iraqi military, forged in the furnace of the ISIS war, had become something its previous incarnation never was: effective.

name: Tongo Tongo Ambush (Niger)
description: On October 4, 2017, a twelve-man US Special Forces team and thirty Nigerien soldiers were ambushed by fifty ISIS-Greater Sahara fighters near the village of Tongo Tongo in southwestern Niger. The patrol had been sent on a reconnaissance mission near the Mali border in unarmored trucks. When the ambush hit, they were outgunned and outmaneuvered in open terrain with no air support for over an hour. Staff Sergeant Bryan Black, Staff Sergeant Jeremiah Johnson, Staff Sergeant Dustin Wright, and Sergeant La David Johnson were killed. Sgt. Johnson's body was not recovered for nearly 48 hours. Most Americans didn't even know US troops were in Niger. The ambush exposed how far the war on terror had spread — quiet deployments across Africa, small teams in remote places, advise-and-assist missions that could turn lethal in seconds. Four men died in a country most people couldn't find on a map, fighting an enemy most people didn't know existed, in a war that had no name and no end.
belligerents: United States Special Forces / Nigerien Armed Forces
result: 4 US soldiers KIA, 2 wounded; 5 Nigerien soldiers KIA

name: Liberation of Hawija
description: October 2017. Three years after ISIS turned Hawija into a stronghold, Iraqi forces finally came to take it back. This was one of the last major ISIS urban positions in Iraq, and the jihadists knew it. They'd had three years to dig in, to mine every road, to rig every building. The Iraqi Counter-Terrorism Service and Army divisions pushed in from multiple axes, supported by coalition airstrikes that turned whole blocks to rubble. The civilians who emerged were ghosts — starved, traumatized, some of them having lived under ISIS rule since 2014. For veterans of Kirkuk province, Hawija's liberation was bittersweet in a way that's hard to explain. The town that had been a thorn in your side for years, the place where you lost friends to IEDs, the district that never stopped resisting — now it was being 'liberated' from a group that made the old insurgents look like amateurs. The cycle of violence in Hawija stretches back to 2003, and anyone honest will tell you it didn't end in 2017 either.
belligerents: Iraqi Security Forces / Coalition air support
result: Iraqi victory — Hawija liberated after three years of ISIS control

name: Battle of Mosul (Liberation from ISIS)
description: October 2016 to July 2017. Nine months. The longest urban battle since World War II. Mosul was ISIS's crown jewel — the city where al-Baghdadi declared his caliphate from the Great Mosque of al-Nuri. Retaking it required 100,000 Iraqi, Kurdish, and militia fighters backed by massive coalition air power. The eastern half fell relatively quickly. The western half — the Old City, with its narrow streets and ancient buildings — was a nightmare. ISIS had tunneled under the entire district. They used civilians as shields. They sent VBIEDs — car bombs — in waves, sometimes ten a day. The Iraqi Counter-Terrorism Service, the Golden Division, took 40% casualties. Forty percent. The coalition flattened entire neighborhoods with airstrikes. The Great Mosque was destroyed. When it was over, parts of western Mosul looked like Stalingrad. For every OIF veteran watching from home, Mosul's liberation was a hall of mirrors — the same city, the same streets, fought over again by a new generation paying the same price.
belligerents: Iraqi Security Forces / Kurdish Peshmerga / Coalition
result: Iraqi / Coalition victory — Mosul liberated, massive destruction

name: Tongo Tongo Ambush
description: October 4, 2017. Tongo Tongo, Niger. A twelve-man ODA from 3rd Special Forces Group and thirty Nigerien soldiers were returning from a reconnaissance mission near the Mali border when they drove into a kill zone. Fifty to one hundred ISIS-Greater Sahara fighters hit them from multiple positions in a treeline. No armored vehicles. No air support — the nearest was over an hour away. No quick reaction force. The team split under fire. Four Green Berets were killed: Staff Sergeant Bryan Black, Staff Sergeant Jeremiah Johnson, Staff Sergeant Dustin Wright, and Sergeant La David Johnson. La David Johnson was separated from the group and found 48 hours later, nearly a mile from the ambush site. He'd fought alone. The ambush exposed what most Americans didn't know: we had troops in Niger. We had troops fighting and dying in West Africa, in a war against ISIS affiliates that Congress hadn't debated and the public hadn't been told about. Four families got the worst knock on their door, and America briefly asked 'wait — we're in Niger?' before moving on. The dead don't get to move on.
belligerents: US 3rd SFG ODA 3212 / Nigerien Armed Forces
result: US/Niger forces ambushed — 4 US KIA, 4 Nigerien KIA

name: Niger-Mali Border Operations
description: The Niger-Mali border region. Sand, scrub, and scattered villages in terrain so vast and so empty that controlling it is more concept than reality. After Tongo Tongo, the operations didn't stop — they got more cautious, more heavily supported, but they continued. US Special Forces and conventional enablers worked alongside Nigerien and French forces in a part of the world where the 'war on terror' looked nothing like Iraq or Afghanistan. No FOBs. No paved roads. No satellite TV in the chow hall. Just small teams living at austere outposts, driving unarmored Toyotas, advising partner forces whose capabilities ranged from professional to non-existent. The threat was ISIS-Greater Sahara to the west and Boko Haram to the southeast, with al-Qaeda affiliates scattered everywhere in between. For anyone deployed to Niger in 2017-2018, the mission was real, the danger was real, and the disconnect between what you were doing and what anyone back home understood about it was total. Africa was the forgotten theater of a forgotten war.
belligerents: US Special Forces / Nigerien Armed Forces / French Forces
result: Ongoing security operations — partners trained, area remained contested

name: Operation Barkhane (French Sahel Operations)
description: The French ran the biggest counterterrorism operation in the Sahel, and if you were deployed to Niger, you worked alongside them. Operation Barkhane — 4,500 French troops spread across five countries, operating from bases in N'Djamena, Niamey, and Gao. The French Foreign Legion and Marine Infantry units were the sharp end, running long-range desert patrols and helicopter assaults against jihadi groups in terrain that makes Afghanistan look lush. They killed senior leaders. They disrupted cells. They fought gun battles in the sand dunes of northern Mali. And they were losing the war. The Sahel was getting worse every year — more groups, more attacks, more territory lost to jihadists. For the Americans working alongside Barkhane, it was a masterclass in the limits of military force: the French were tactically superb and strategically stuck. You can't kill your way out of a problem rooted in state collapse, poverty, and ethnic grievance. By 2022, the French would be kicked out of Mali by a military junta that preferred Russian mercenaries. The whole thing unraveled. But in 2017-2018, the partnership was real, the missions were real, and the French bled alongside us.
belligerents: French Armed Forces / Sahel partner nations
ISGS / JNIM / Ansar Dine / various jihadi groups
result: Ongoing operations — tactical successes but strategic deterioration

name: Battle of Raqqa
description: June to October 2017. The 'capital' of the ISIS caliphate. The city where they filmed beheadings, where they planned the Paris attacks, where they built their slave markets. The Syrian Democratic Forces — primarily Kurdish YPG fighters with embedded US Special Operations advisors — went in to end it. Four months of house-to-house combat through a city that ISIS had spent three years fortifying. Tunnels under every neighborhood. IEDs in every building. Snipers in minarets. Suicide bombers on every street corner. The SDF lost over 600 fighters. Civilian casualties were devastating — the UN estimated that coalition airstrikes killed hundreds. The city was gutted. When the last ISIS fighters surrendered in the stadium in October, Raqqa looked like Dresden. The caliphate's capital was rubble. For anyone who'd watched ISIS metastasize from Iraq to Syria and back, who'd watched them take Mosul and Kirkuk and Raqqa, the fall of their so-called capital felt like a turning point. The caliphate was dead. But the ideology that built it was just as alive as the day they raised their black flag.
belligerents: Syrian Democratic Forces / US Special Operations / Coalition air

name: Agadez Air Base Operations (Niger)
description: Agadez, northern Niger. The US built a $110 million drone base here — Air Base 201 — in the middle of the Sahara, and most Americans never knew it existed. The base was the centerpiece of the US intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance architecture in West Africa, flying MQ-9 Reapers over the vast Saharan spaces where jihadi groups trained, moved, and planned. For the airmen and soldiers deployed to Agadez, it was austere duty in the extreme — desert heat, sandstorms, minimal amenities, and a mission that involved staring at screens for hours tracking targets in some of the most remote terrain on earth. The base was controversial with Nigerien politicians and locals who didn't want an American military presence in their backyard. The airmen who served there knew they were in the other war — the one fought with drones and signals intelligence and partner-force training in a region that was slowly being consumed by jihadi insurgencies that nobody back home was paying attention to. Agadez was the future of American warfare: small footprint, low visibility, high stakes, and zero public awareness.
belligerents: ISIS-GS / JNIM / various Saharan jihadi groups
result: Operational ISR hub established — significant intelligence gathering

name: 3rd SFG Sahel Deployment
description: 2017-2018. The 3rd Special Forces Group's deployment to the Sahel — the one that included the Tongo Tongo patrol — was the tip of a much larger American presence in West Africa that most people never knew about. ODAs spread across Niger, Cameroon, Chad, and other nations, training partner forces, building relationships with local military leaders, and conducting patrols in some of the most ungoverned space on earth. These weren't the door-kicking raids of Iraq and Afghanistan. This was the slow, patient work of unconventional warfare: learning languages, understanding tribal dynamics, earning trust in communities where the US military was an alien concept. The Green Berets lived in austere conditions, often in tents or local compounds, with minimal support and long drives through terrain where a breakdown could leave you stranded for days. After Tongo Tongo, the mission changed — more force protection, more restrictions, more oversight. The freewheeling partner-force model that had defined the Sahel mission was reined in. But the mission didn't stop. The quiet war in West Africa continued, invisible to all but the families of the people fighting it.
result: Partner forces trained — mission parameters tightened after Tongo Tongo

name: Battle of Eastern Ghouta
description: Four hundred thousand people trapped in a pocket the size of a small suburb, being shelled around the clock. That was Eastern Ghouta — close enough to Damascus that you could see Assad's palace from the rooftops, and far enough from the world's conscience that nobody stopped it. Then came Douma, and the images of children foaming at the mouth from what looked like a chemical attack. The West launched token missile strikes. Assad kept bombing. Ghouta fell. The message was clear: outrage has an expiration date, and dictators know it.

name: Turkish Operation Olive Branch (Afrin)
description: Turkey looked at the Kurds who'd been America's best allies against ISIS and decided they were a bigger threat than the caliphate ever was. Turkish tanks and F-16s, alongside Syrian rebel proxies, rolled into Kurdish Afrin and took it in two months. Over 100,000 Kurds fled their homes. The same Kurdish fighters who'd bled at Kobani and Raqqa watched their own territory seized by a NATO ally. The betrayal was so brazen it barely made the news cycle. In Syria, loyalty is the cheapest commodity of all.

name: Kerch Strait Incident
description: Russian coast guard vessels rammed and opened fire on three small Ukrainian naval boats trying to pass through the Kerch Strait — under a bridge that Russia had built across Ukrainian waters to connect to its stolen Crimea. The audacity was stunning: Russia was now physically blocking Ukraine's access to its own coastline and shooting at anyone who objected. Three boats captured, sailors wounded, and the world did nothing. Looking back, the Kerch Strait incident was the moment Russia confirmed that force was the only language it would speak. Four years later, the full conversation began.

name: Menaka Region Operations (Mali-Niger Border)
description: The Menaka region of eastern Mali, right up against the Niger border. This was the operational backyard for anyone deployed to western Niger — the area where ISIS-Greater Sahara recruited, trained, and launched the kind of attacks that killed four Americans at Tongo Tongo. French Barkhane forces and Malian troops operated in the region with US intelligence support, trying to disrupt the jihadi networks that flowed back and forth across borders that existed on maps but not on the ground. The nomadic Tuareg and Fulani populations of the region were caught between competing armed groups — JNIM, ISGS, government militias, and tribal self-defense forces — in a conflict so complex that the 'good guys' and 'bad guys' changed depending on which village you were in. For the Americans operating in this space, Menaka was a lesson in the limits of the partner-force model: you can train and equip and advise, but you can't impose order on a region where the state has never been present and where the population has learned to survive by accommodation rather than resistance.
belligerents: French Barkhane / Malian Army / US ISR support
result: Ongoing contested security — jihadi presence persistent

name: Battle of Khasham (US vs Russian Mercenaries)
description: February 7, 2018. Deir ez-Zor province, Syria. A force of 500 pro-Assad fighters, including Russian Wagner Group mercenaries, attacked a position held by US Special Operations forces and their SDF allies near Khasham. What happened next was a one-sided annihilation. US forces called in every asset available: F-15Es, B-52s, AC-130 gunships, Apache helicopters, and HIMARS rocket artillery. Over four hours, the attacking force was systematically destroyed. Estimates range from 100 to 300 killed, including a significant number of Russian mercenaries. Moscow denied their presence. The Wagner Group families who never got their sons back know better. Khasham was a moment where the shadow war between the US and Russia in Syria briefly became a shooting war — and the US won so decisively that Russia pretended it never happened. For everyone watching the Syria campaign unfold, Khasham was the proof that American firepower, when unleashed without political restraint, is still the most lethal force on the planet. The Russians found out the hard way.
belligerents: US Special Operations / SDF / US Air Force
Pro-Assad forces / Russian Wagner Group mercenaries
warName: War against ISIS / Syrian Civil War

name: Battle of Baghuz
description: The caliphate that once stretched from Aleppo to the outskirts of Baghdad had been compressed into a filthy tent encampment on a bend of the Euphrates River. Thousands of emaciated ISIS fighters and their families — women in black, hollow-eyed children — staggered out of Baghuz into SDF custody. Five years earlier, al-Baghdadi had declared himself caliph of all Muslims from a golden pulpit. Now his empire was a bombed-out sandbar. The physical caliphate was dead. The idea, unfortunately, wasn't.
result: SDF/Coalition victory; end of ISIS territorial caliphate

name: Killing of Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi
description: On October 26, 2019, Delta Force operators flew eight helicopters into Barisha, Syria — deep into Idlib Province, controlled by al-Qaeda-linked groups — to kill the man who had declared himself caliph of the Islamic State. Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi, who had stood in the pulpit of Mosul's Great Mosque in 2014 and proclaimed dominion over all Muslims, was cornered in a tunnel beneath a compound. He detonated a suicide vest, killing himself and three of his children. The operators' military working dog, Conan, chased him into the tunnel. The raid required flying through Russian and Turkish-controlled airspace, deconflicting with multiple hostile parties, and hitting a target in territory where everyone on the ground was an enemy. Baghdadi had overseen a reign of genocide against the Yazidis, systematic rape slavery, mass executions, and the destruction of civilizations that had existed for millennia. He died in a hole, covered in rubble, wearing a suicide vest. The caliphate died in a tunnel.
result: Al-Baghdadi killed via suicide vest detonation during raid

name: Manbij Bombing — Green Village
description: On January 16, 2019, an ISIS suicide bomber detonated in a restaurant near the Green Village base in Manbij, Syria, killing four Americans: Army Chief Warrant Officer 2 Jonathan Farmer, 37. Navy Chief Cryptologic Technician Shannon Kent, 35 — a mother of two who spoke six languages and had deployed five times. DoD civilian Scott Wirtz, a former Navy SEAL. And Ghadir Taher, a Syrian-born American interpreter. The bombing came weeks after President Trump announced a US withdrawal from Syria, declaring ISIS defeated. Four Americans died proving the war wasn't over. Shannon Kent was one of the most decorated women in special operations — multiple Bronze Stars, combat deployments across the GWOT — and she was killed by a teenager in a suicide vest in a war her president had just declared finished. The gap between Washington's narrative and the reality on the ground was measured in body bags.
result: 4 Americans KIA, 3 US service members wounded

name: Death of Baghdadi (Barisha Raid)
description: October 26, 2019. Barisha, Idlib province, Syria. Delta Force operators fly eight helicopters through some of the most contested airspace on earth — Turkish, Syrian, Russian, and various militia air defense zones — to a compound near the Turkish border. Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi, the self-proclaimed Caliph of the Islamic State, the man who stood in the Great Mosque of al-Nuri and declared dominion over every Muslim on earth, runs into a dead-end tunnel with two of his children and detonates a suicide vest. The tunnel collapses. Baghdadi is identified by DNA taken from the remains. A military working dog named Conan pursued him into the tunnel and was injured. The raid was the bookend to a campaign that had consumed five years, involved dozens of nations, and killed hundreds of thousands of people. The caliphate was already rubble. Now its caliph was dead. For every veteran of the ISIS war — for everyone who watched Kirkuk threatened, Mosul fall, Sinjar burn — Baghdadi's death was satisfying in the way that killing a symptom of a disease is satisfying. The man was gone. The conditions that created him were not.
belligerents: US Delta Force / SOAR / US Air Force
Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi / ISIS security detail

name: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War
description: Every general on the planet should have been watching Nagorno-Karabakh, because the future of war was being written there in 44 days. Turkish-made Bayraktar drones and Israeli kamikaze munitions hunted Armenian tanks, artillery, and air defenses with the patient, clinical precision of a video game — except the people inside those vehicles were real. Armenia's Soviet-era military was systematically dismantled by technology that cost a fraction of the hardware it destroyed. The drone age didn't start here, but this is where everyone finally noticed.

name: Battle of Shusha
description: When the drones couldn't do it, Azerbaijani special forces did it the old-fashioned way — climbing sheer cliffs at night to assault a fortress city that sits on a mountaintop like something out of Lord of the Rings. Three days of hand-to-hand fighting through the streets of Shusha, and when it fell, the war was over. Armenia signed the ceasefire within hours. Thirty years of frozen conflict, resolved in 44 days by a combination of ancient courage and modern technology.

name: Battle of Mekelle (2020)
description: Ethiopia's prime minister — a Nobel Peace Prize winner, of all things — sent the federal army and Eritrean allies into Tigray after the TPLF struck first. Mekelle fell fast, and the government declared victory. But the TPLF melted into the countryside, launched a guerrilla campaign that recaptured the capital within months, and triggered a humanitarian catastrophe that killed hundreds of thousands through famine and violence. The Nobel laureate's quick war became Africa's deadliest 21st-century conflict.
result: Ethiopian/Eritrean capture; TPLF recaptured it in 2021

name: Battle of Idlib (2019-2020)
description: Russia and Syria went for the kill in Idlib — the last rebel stronghold, three million people crammed into a shrinking pocket. Then a Russian or Syrian airstrike killed 34 Turkish soldiers, and Erdogan unleashed his drones. Turkish Bayraktars devastated Syrian government armor and artillery columns in a 72-hour blitz that looked like a preview of Nagorno-Karabakh. Suddenly it was Turkey versus Russia in a proxy knife fight, and both sides blinked. The ceasefire held. Idlib survived. Barely.

name: Nagorno-Karabakh Drone War
description: The Armenians had tanks, artillery, air defenses — a conventional army built over thirty years. The Azerbaijanis had Turkish drones that cost a fraction of a single tank. And the drones won.
